metadata.positioning: triple-track learning app for shell, C, Python and AI literacy

tiers.label: Official 42 sources
tiers.confidence_rationale: Published and maintained by 42 Network or 42 Lausanne directly.

tiers.label: Community docs and guides
tiers.confidence_rationale: Written by experienced students or educators. Useful for explanation but may contain outdated or campus-specific information.
examples: ArchWiki and Ubuntu tutorials for Linux concepts

tiers.label: Testers and tooling
tiers.confidence_rationale: Community-maintained test suites. Reliable for behavioral checks but not authoritative on project requirements — the subject PDF is.

tiers.label: Solution repositories metadata
tiers.confidence_rationale: Useful to know which projects exist and their structure, but solution code must never be surfaced as learning material.
examples: GitHub repository names and descriptions for 42 projects
README files describing project scope and constraints
File structure patterns (e.g. Makefile, src/, includes/)

tiers.label: Direct solution content
tiers.confidence_rationale: Solution code is unverified, may violate 42 norms, and showing it defeats the learning model. Blocked in foundation and practice phases.
examples: Function implementations from student solution repos
Copy-paste code from get_next_line, ft_printf, push_swap solutions
AI-generated complete solutions to 42 project requirements

fallback_rules.description: When no official 42 source covers a topic, fall back to community_docs but mark the information as community-sourced and medium confidence.
When community sources disagree with each other, prefer the source closer to the official 42 ecosystem (e.g. 42docs over a personal blog) and flag the uncertainty.
When neither official nor community sources cover a topic, state clearly that the information is inferred and should be verified by the learner.
In foundation or practice phases, never provide complete solutions even if available. Offer observation, question, hint and next action instead.

confidence_scale.high: Published by 42 Network or campus. Treat as ground truth.
confidence_scale.medium: Community-vetted, generally reliable. Cross-check when precision matters.
confidence_scale.low: Useful as metadata only. Do not treat as authoritative content.
confidence_scale.inferred: Not verified against any source. Must be flagged explicitly to the learner.

tracks.title: Shell 0 to Hero
tracks.summary: Linux-first recovery track before Piscine pressure.
tracks.why_it_matters: Rebuild confidence, command fluency, filesystem intuition and process awareness.

modules.title: Navigation and files
modules.deliverable: Navigate, create, copy and clean files without hesitation.

modules.title: Redirections and pipes
modules.deliverable: Compose useful shell chains and inspect text quickly.

modules.title: Permissions and execution
modules.deliverable: Understand why a script runs or fails.

modules.title: Linux work habits
modules.deliverable: Operate daily in Linux without depending on GUI reflexes.

tracks.title: C / Core 42
tracks.summary: Low-level rigor track aligned with the core 42 mindset.
tracks.why_it_matters: Build algorithmic reasoning, memory discipline and debugging habits.

modules.title: Syntax and control flow
modules.deliverable: Write small programs cleanly and compile with warnings enabled.

modules.title: Pointers and memory
modules.deliverable: Explain memory decisions and avoid basic leaks.

modules.title: Library and algorithm bridge
modules.deliverable: Prepare for libft, printf, gnl and push_swap logic.

tracks.title: Python + AI
tracks.summary: Python foundations plus the modern AI axis now present in 42 Lausanne.
tracks.why_it_matters: Prepare for the new branch without weakening shell and C fundamentals.

modules.title: Python foundations
modules.deliverable: Write and explain small scripts confidently.

modules.title: Objects and automation
modules.deliverable: Build useful utilities and simple models.

modules.title: AI, RAG and agent literacy
modules.deliverable: Use AI as a disciplined tool without outsourcing understanding.

legacy_common_core.summary: Historically C and Unix heavy, then concurrency, graphics, C++, network and devops.

new_common_core_interpretation.summary: Algorithms earlier, Python earlier, AI inside the common core, still grounded in systems thinking.
new_common_core_interpretation.note: Project names beyond official public category statements come from infographic interpretation and community mapping.
